title: Rally Dev — Scope Model, Work Items, Permissions
topic: Rally Dev scope model: validate Vector PLA-0042 → PLA-0043 direction
summary: How Rally Dev sets project scope, attaches work items, and allocates user rights — used to validate Vector's ?scope=<node_id> approach.
content:
Product Overview

Rally (originally Rally Software, rebranded CA Agile Central under CA Technologies, now Broadcom Rally) is an enterprise-scale agile planning platform acquired by Broadcom as part of its CA Technologies acquisition. It currently sits within Broadcom's ValueOps suite alongside Clarity (portfolio/programme management), ConnectALL (integration), and Insights (analytics). Rally is available as SaaS and, since 2024, as Rally Anywhere — an on-premises offering. Pricing is not publicly listed; enterprise licensing is sold by quote. The product is positioned at large-scale organisations with hundreds of teams and complex delivery hierarchies.

Scope Model

Hierarchy: Subscription → Workspace → Project (tree)

The Rally data model has three containment levels:

LevelDescriptionAdmin role
SubscriptionThe top billing/licensing envelope. One subscription can contain multiple workspaces.Subscription Admin
WorkspaceAn isolated reporting domain. Projects, custom fields, schedule-state names, and portfolio-item type names are all workspace-scoped. Workspaces cannot see each other's data.Workspace Admin
Project (tree)A node in a multi-level tree within a workspace. Represents one team or organisational unit. Projects may have child projects to arbitrary depth. Projects must reside in the same workspace to be related.Project Admin

UI Scope Control — the Project Picker

The primary scope-selection control is the Project Picker dropdown, located in the upper-left corner of every Rally page (global, persistent across the session). It displays a tree of projects in the current workspace. Below the project tree the picker renders two optional checkboxes:

  • Parent Projects (scope-up) — include matching work items from ancestor projects above the selection.
  • Child Projects (scope-down) — include matching work items from all descendant projects below the selection (defaults to on).

An on-screen indicator below the picker shows the active scope combination. Selecting a parent project node and enabling "Child Projects" is the canonical way for a programme manager to see all child-team work items rolled up in a single view.

Scope Persistence — session + user profile, not URL

Rally persists scope through server-side user-profile preferences, not URL query parameters. Users configure their default Workspace and Project via avatar → My Settings → Edit Profile → Settings → Workspace / Project. On login the saved preference is applied; the picker reflects it. The App SDK internally tracks projectScopeUp and projectScopeDown as boolean state on the ProjectPicker component (stored in Rally.data.ModelFactory on the WSAPI model layer), not in the URL. Browser-cache/cookie clearing can be required for profile changes to take effect across sessions.

API Scope Parameters

While the UI does not expose scope via URL query strings, the Web Services API (WSAPI) makes scope explicit on every request:

GET https://rally1.rallydev.com/slm/webservice/v2.0/hierarchicalrequirement
  ?workspace=/workspace/{workspaceOID}
  &project=/project/{projectOID}
  &projectScopeUp=false
  &projectScopeDown=true

These four parameters are orthogonal: workspace pins the workspace; project pins the anchor project node; projectScopeUp (default false) extends upward; projectScopeDown (default true) extends downward. Passing project=null scopes to the entire workspace. The Lookback (Snapshot) API uses the workspace OID in the URL path and filters by _ProjectHierarchy (an array field containing all ancestor project OIDs) or Project (direct assignment OID).

Work Item Model

Artifact types and their project FK

Every artifact (restricted entity) in Rally carries a hard Project foreign key — a reference to the single project node the item "belongs to". This is system-assigned when the item is created (defaults to the active scope project) and can be changed. There is no multi-project membership for a single artifact at rest.

Artifact typeProject FKIteration FKRelease FKParent FKNotes
User Story (HierarchicalRequirement)RequiredOptionalOptionalPortfolio Item or parent StoryScheduling into an Iteration removes it from Backlog view
DefectRequiredOptionalOptional—Same schema shape as User Story for scoping
TaskRequired (inherited from parent Story)——User Story (required)Always co-located with parent Story's project
Feature (Portfolio Item L1)Required—OptionalInitiative (Portfolio Item L2)Child Stories may live in different projects
Initiative (Portfolio Item L2)Required——Theme (Portfolio Item L3, if configured)Default top-most portfolio tier in most Rally installs
Capability / ThemeRequired———Workspace-configurable portfolio item tiers

Cross-project parent-child semantics

Rally explicitly supports cross-project parenting: a Feature stored in a "strategy" Programme project can have child User Stories stored in separate "execution" child-team projects. The recommended pattern is a strategy project at a high node (holding portfolio items) with execution projects as children (holding stories). When a user scopes to the strategy project with Child Projects enabled, Rally rolls up all child-team stories under the programme portfolio items automatically. Child stories do not need to be in the same project as their parent Feature — this is the core cross-project linking mechanic.

Rollup semantics

Rollups are automatic when projects share identically-named Iterations and Releases (same name, same start/end dates). Capacity, task actuals, and velocity aggregate upward through matching timeboxes. There is no explicit "rollup flag" — it is purely driven by timebox name matching across the hierarchy.

Permission Model

Role registry

Role nameScopeKey capabilities
Subscription AdministratorEntire subscriptionAll capabilities of all lower roles. Creates/edits users, sets password policies, manages billing, creates workspaces, delegates user management to Workspace and Project Admins, resets passwords (only role that can), manages licence seats, adds subscription-wide custom user fields.
Workspace AdministratorSingle workspaceFull access to all work items in the workspace. Customises workspace settings (schedule state names, portfolio item type names, tags, custom fields, work rules). Adds/removes users from the workspace and its projects. Cannot edit a Subscription Admin's record.
Project AdministratorSingle project (and any child projects where granted)Administers project settings. Can create child projects. Manages Viewer / Editor / Team Member permissions within the project. Can add existing workspace users to the project. May be granted user-creation rights by subscription admin (not default).
EditorSingle projectCreates, edits, and deletes work items. Manages milestones. Can create and accept tasks.
Team MemberSingle projectAll Editor capabilities. Additionally appears in owner-assignment dropdowns and on the Team Status page. Being a Team Member does not block non-team-members from being assigned work items.
ViewerSingle projectRead-only access to the project and all work items within it.
User (workspace level)WorkspaceHas workspace access but no project access until explicitly added to individual projects. Adding a user to any project automatically grants workspace-level User access.
No AccessWorkspace or projectExplicitly blocked from seeing the workspace or project.

Permission inheritance and cascading

Rally does not cascade permissions automatically down the project tree. A user granted Editor on a parent project is not automatically an Editor on child projects — each project's permissions are set independently. Workspace Admin status is the one exception: it grants full access to all projects within that workspace without per-project assignment. The PermissionMap API manages access via four categorical lists: EditorProjects, AdminProjects, MembershipProjects, ViewerProjects (and corresponding revocation lists: NoAccessProjects, NoMembershipProjects), reflecting the flat per-project grant model. Workspace-level lists are AdminWorkspaces and UserWorkspaces.

A user with lesser permissions cannot view or edit a user with greater permissions (e.g. Workspace Admin cannot see a Subscription Admin's record). Default workspace permissions can be pre-configured by Subscription/Workspace Admins so new users are automatically granted a baseline role on all projects in that workspace.

Comparison Take

Rally's scope mechanism is fundamentally session-state + API parameter, not URL query-param. The UI picker sets a server-side preference (workspace OID + project OID + scopeUp/Down booleans) that persists in the user's profile; individual pages reload against that stored context without encoding scope in the address bar. The WSAPI exposes scope explicitly as ?workspace=&project=&projectScopeUp=&projectScopeDown= on REST calls, making every read unambiguously scoped. Vector's ?scope=<node_id> approach for PLA-0043 maps well to the Rally API model and is arguably more transparent — encoding scope in the URL means bookmarks and shared links carry scope, which Rally's session model does not support. The key Rally insight worth carrying forward is the scope-up / scope-down duality: Vector's current ?scope= parameter assumes a single node; Rally's model shows that real-world users need to choose whether to roll up to ancestor nodes or drill down to child nodes independently of the anchor. Whether Vector adds explicit &scopeDown=true / &scopeUp=false query flags or bakes child rollup in by convention is a design decision — Rally chose explicit opt-in for scope-up (off by default) and opt-in for scope-down (on by default), which matches the most common "show me my team plus everything below" use case.

Gaps and Limitations

  • URL scope encoding in the Rally SaaS UI — All available docs confirm scope is session/profile-stored, not URL-encoded in the browser address bar. No Rally doc explicitly states "there is no ?project= in the UI URL" — this is inferred from the absence of any such reference across all SDK, community, and API docs reviewed.
  • Portfolio item Project FK — required vs optional — Docs confirm every artifact has a Project field but do not state whether it is required (non-null) for portfolio items at creation time. It is system-assigned and changeable post-creation.
  • Permission inheritance edge cases — No doc explicitly describes what happens when a Project Admin creates a child project: does the Admin automatically get Project Admin on the child? Likely yes, but not confirmed.
  • Pricing tiers — No public pricing table found. Enterprise quote-only.
  • Rally Anywhere (on-prem, 2024) — Scope/permission model is assumed to match SaaS; no on-prem-specific scoping docs reviewed.
  • login-walled pages — The actual Rally SaaS UI and some older help.rallydev.com pages redirect to the Broadcom TechDocs landing page and could not be crawled directly. All findings draw from public TechDocs, Knowledge Base, and community threads only.
